coredata.dc:title: Wire bond contact defect identification in battery modules of electric vehicles using pulses and differential voltage analysis

coredata.dc:creator: Ank, Manuel
Brehler, Tobias
Lienkamp, Markus
coredata.dc:description: Automotive battery packs for electromobility applications consist of a large number of interconnected battery cells. Different cell-to-busbar joining techniques are utilized, with cylindrical cells frequently being contacted using wire bonding. Failure of individual connections can occur due to strong vibrations during operation and improper stress, making detection by the battery management system a necessity. This study investigates the identification of an electrical wire bond failure in a state-of-the-art electric vehicle module of a Lucid Air with 10 series-connected and 30 parallel-connected cells (10s30p). Four individual cells were characterized extensively in order to generate a simulation model taking into account parameter scatter. The failure case under investigation was simulatively incorporated in one parallel circuit and subsequently replicated in experimental validation measurements at the module level. The results show that this defect can be detected using pulses of C/3 or higher currents at various states of charge. An even more robust detection is achieved using differential voltage analysis of constant current C/20 discharge voltage trajectories. This defect identification method does not require any additional measurement sensors beyond the voltage taps and sensors provided by the manufacturer – with one voltage sensor per parallel circuit – and can therefore be implemented during electric vehicle usage, e.g. at dedicated service checks. A discussion on the applicability and scalability as well as the limitations of the method is provided. All measurement data of the state-of-the-art Lucid battery system is available as open source alongside the article.

head.ce:title: Wire bond contact defect identification in battery modules of electric vehicles using pulses and differential voltage analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technical University of Munich (TUM), School of Engineering & Design, Department of Mobility Systems Engineering, Institute of Automotive Technology, Boltzmannstr. 15, Garching, D-85748, Germany

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: M.A. and T.B. contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Automotive battery packs for electromobility applications consist of a large number of interconnected battery cells. Different cell-to-busbar joining techniques are utilized, with cylindrical cells frequently being contacted using wire bonding. Failure of individual connections can occur due to strong vibrations during operation and improper stress, making detection by the battery management system a necessity. This study investigates the identification of an electrical wire bond failure in a state-of-the-art electric vehicle module of a Lucid Air with 10 series-connected and 30 parallel-connected cells (10s30p). Four individual cells were characterized extensively in order to generate a simulation model taking into account parameter scatter. The failure case under investigation was simulatively incorporated in one parallel circuit and subsequently replicated in experimental validation measurements at the module level. The results show that this defect can be detected using pulses of C/3 or higher currents at various states of charge. An even more robust detection is achieved using differential voltage analysis of constant current C/20 discharge voltage trajectories. This defect identification method does not require any additional measurement sensors beyond the voltage taps and sensors provided by the manufacturer – with one voltage sensor per parallel circuit – and can therefore be implemented during electric vehicle usage, e.g. at dedicated service checks. A discussion on the applicability and scalability as well as the limitations of the method is provided. All measurement data of the state-of-the-art Lucid battery system is available as open source alongside the article.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The measurement data of the experimental procedures are provided as open source alongside the article at https://doi.org/10.14459/2023mp1715739.

ce:section.ce:para: In order for electric vehicles to meet performance and range requirements, their battery system consists of a large number of electrically series-connected and usually also parallel-connected battery cells [1–3]. The connections can be damaged due to corrosion, deformation or vibration and – depending on the contacting process – may even fail completely [4–6]. An increase in electrical contact resistance due to a faulty contact causes not only reduced vehicle performance and range but also an enhanced risk of a thermal runaway, which is why the detection of defective contacts is of utmost importance.

Various requirements exist for the interconnection of battery cells, since electrical contacting with low contact resistance is to be ensured while securing the cells with sufficient strength — without mechanical fatigue, corrosion or rust (as well as with low thermal stress during the joining process) [7,8]. If one or more requirements have not been fulfilled, the result cannot only be a limited performance but also a complete loss of function of the connection and thus a failure of the battery system due to the various load conditions in the vehicle [9].

The interconnection of several cells is achieved with busbars made of aluminum or copper, whereby an increase in the cross-section leads to a lower maximum temperature during operation as well as electrical resistance — at higher mass and cost [10]. On the one hand, resistance spot welding, laser welding, soldering, brazing and ultrasonic welding have established themselves as common contacting methods for cell contacting applications [7,11,12]. On the other hand, cylindrical cells of limited capacity are frequently contacted using wire bonding, with the welded bonding wires serving as fuses and thus increasing safety [7,12]. Depending on the cross-section, the resistance of the wires is rather high with the fragile bonds (low wire strength) susceptible to breakage [7]. Such damage will affect the performance of the battery pack, since the bonding wires itself typically lack redundancy, in contrast to e.g. laser welds. In rare cases, friction-locked, detachable connections such as screw connections and press contacts can also be found — especially when serviceability, repair options and recycling are paramount [13,14]. In summary, the cell-to-busbar connections present in state-of-the-art electric vehicles can fundamentally be classified into direct and indirect techniques. Fig. 1 presents an overview of these methods including schematics, characteristics and examples of state-of-the-art series production vehicle modules. For a detailed review of the contacting procedures, the reader is referred to publications by Zwicker et al. [7], Das et al. [8] and Lee et al. [14].

When designing the contacting technology for battery packs, the automation capability, the efficiency of the processes and the relationship to the internal resistance of the cell are relevant in addition to the absolute value of the contact resistance [15]. Furthermore, it is necessary to take an uneven distribution of currents into account, both due to cell scattering and as a result of different and partially parasitic cell temperatures [15–19].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential cell and module level defects
ce:section.ce:para: Thermal, chemical, electrical and kinetic risks can emerge from the energy storage of battery electric vehicles (BEVs): If the temperature rises sharply due to an uncontrolled release of energy, thermal hazards result, while the potential leakage of electrolyte poses a chemical risk due to the electrolyte salt reacting with humidity as well as, if utilized, the flammability and toxicity of organic compounds [20]. An electrical risk can arise due to the high current capability and the partially high voltage level depending on the topology in the battery system, whereas a kinetic risk ensues in the event of an explosion due to cell excess pressure [20]. Hazard-causing events in the automotive context include cell-internal faults, vehicle crashes, assembly faults and incorrect operating limits, with risks at cell level arising from deformations, intrusions, under- or overvoltages, internal or external short circuits, over- or deep discharge and heat [21].

At the cell level, thermo-mechanical stresses or vibrations were identified to cause risks, e.g. detachment of the welded joint of the main cell conductor, which could lead to arcing and thus to a thermal runaway [22]. Vehicle recalls have also been issued due to bent conductor foils in production, torn tabs of the current collector at the anode and contamination of the electrodes with particles from manufacturing — potentially leading to internal short circuits [23–25]. The alleged contact of cathode tabs with anode electrode material due to production process deviations at the supplier has furthermore already led to vehicle safety recalls [26]. According to Marlair et al. [27], the presence of non-intentionally added substances during manufacturing is one major reason for recalls, along with substandard design.

At the module level, potential defects arise if screwed module connectors are utilized, which can loosen in the event of improper assembly or due to the screw-setting behavior [28]. The cell interconnection is another possible source of failure due to joining processes, as an unreliable connection of solder joints can lead to a spontaneous drop in drive power [29]. Deformations as a result of accidents, water ingress due to insufficient sealing of the battery pack or coolant spills due to leaks can lead to an external short circuit of cells or entire modules [30]. Furthermore, the functionality of the overall system may be limited due to a faulty battery management system (BMS) [31,32]. A consideration of the recalls related to low-voltage electrics indicates that manual work steps, in particular, are prone to errors (interchanged plugs, forgotten components, faulty screw connections) [33–36].

In general, fault diagnosis for lithium-ion batteries is challenging, since many different fault cases may occur with different causes having the same consequence (for example, increased resistance). In the following, an overview of methods for the detection of faults is given, with a specific focus on faulty contacts and connectors, since these increase the ohmic resistance in the case of incremental failure and can thus cause critical heating of the battery.

Ma et al. [37] suggested a redundant, cross-voltage measurement in order to be able to clearly attribute an increased resistance to the internal resistance or the contacting or the connector (Fig. 2a). The evaluation of the voltage signals was performed statistically. Yang et al. [38] similarly used cross-voltage measurements, but preprocessed the data with a recursive Pearson correlation coefficient, extracted fault-related features with wavelet packet decomposition and classified the fault mode and degree using an artificial neural network (ANN) and a multi-classification relevance vector machine (RVM). In this way, defective sensors could be detected in addition to defective cells and connectors — with a success rate of up to 98% [38]. Yu et al. [39] compared two new methods based on Euclidean distance and cosine similarity with a method based on correlation coefficients: All three methods are suitable for detecting defective connectors, sensor faults, as well as short circuits; however, there are differences in sensitivity, robustness, and detection time. Kang et al. used a redundant interleaved connected voltage measurement method in a first publication ([40]) and a non-redundant crossed-style measurement circuit with an analysis method based on correlation coefficients in another publication ([41]). Hashemi et al. [42] developed an adaptive battery model based on a second order equivalent circuit model (ECM) in which models’ parameters are estimated by a supervised machine learning paradigm to increase the accuracy of BMS functions — such as fault detection. No cross-cell defects and no system level were considered, however. Yang et al. [43] proposed an online multi-fault diagnosis method to identify undervoltage, overvoltage, connection faults, and sensor faults. The methodology is based on the comparison of voltage envelopes, using similarity to detect the presence of a fault and polarity to determine the fault type. The four types of faults in question could be correctly identified in the recordings of a 5s1p circuit (nomenclature in this work: XsNp with X cells connected in series and N cells connected in parallel) [43].

Since no cross-voltage measurement is available in current electric vehicles (Fig. 2b), procedures are presented in the following that can be deployed on series-production hardware. However, voltage recording which includes the connector and contact resistors within the potential difference is a prerequisite. Shang et al. [44] used a modified sample entropy to detect faults within a series circuit. The fault cases were limited to overvoltages and undervoltages of the cells — connection faults were not investigated. Yao et al. [45] investigated defective connectors by means of entropy: the voltages of serially connected cells were recorded and evaluated under vibration load. Using a discrete cosine transform, the voltage signal was filtered to remove system and measurement noise and fault diagnosis were performed using local Shannon entropy, ensemble Shannon entropy, and sample entropy. The increased resistance due to a loosened nut at one of the cell terminals could be detected with the ensemble Shannon entropy and the sample entropy [45]. Ma et al. [46] presented a method to detect faults with the help of a principal component analysis (PCA) model of the battery. The faulty waveform of the battery parameters including ohmic resistance, terminal voltage, and open-circuit voltage was reconstructed by means of parallel kernel principal component analysis (KPCA) in order to investigate the degree and the cause of the fault. Zheng et al. [4] observed a power fade fault in a BEV with 96s1p topology, aggregated the individual cell and contact resistances and compared these total resistances after considering a linear temperature compensation over 3 months of driving data. Based on the Shannon entropy, the increase in resistance could be attributed to a loosened screw connection, with the fault being rectified by re-tightening the connection, thus verifying the detection [4]. Lin et al. [47] developed a diagnostic method based on correlation coefficients, taking inconsistencies in resistance as well as the state of charge (state of charge (SOC)) into account. This improved the accuracy and speed of the diagnosis and enabled the detection of defective voltage sensors, connections as well as short circuits. Yao et al. [48] monitored and evaluated voltage data from a battery pack, filtering the data and extracting the (horizontal) variance and covariance as features. This served as input for the optimization of an extreme learning machine (ELM) with genetic algorithms — though no information on detected error cases is available. Xie et al. [49] developed a diagnostic framework to identify cell faults (internal and external short circuit, overheating) within a series-connection: The voltage synchronicity between adjacent cells was quantified using a recursive correlation coefficient; then the correlation coefficient series was converted into pseudo images (using Gramian Angular Field (GAF) and Markov Transition Field (MTF) transformations) and searched for fault symptoms using a convolutional neural network (CNN) [49]. It is not known whether the approach can be extended to defective cell contacts. Li et al. [50] used a combination of a long short-term memory (LSTM) recurrent neural network and an ECM to develop a fault diagnosis method for electric vehicles. However, all single-cell voltages were available for the analysis and no parallel circuits were present in the evaluated real-world operation data set — thus the transferability to systems with parallel circuits is uncertain.

Fewer research has been devoted to the identification of faults in parallel connections: Pan et al. [51] detected internal short circuits within parallel connections, although they used two additional Ampere meters not present in series-production vehicles. Song et al. [52] studied parallel-connected cells with a common current and voltage sensor. The resistance of the entire parallel circuit (determined by sinusoidal current excitation at 0.5Hz) served as the health indicator, while an increase of at least 60% of the ohmic resistance was considered a cell error. The contact resistance was neglected. By means of a statistical evaluation of resistances of pristine as well as aged cells, thresholds were defined by which defects are classified as such [52]. The choice of these limits was crucial, as a too high threshold value would lead to undetected defects and a too low threshold value would cause cells to be classified as defective due to their parameter variations. The method provided good results for pristine cells (with small fluctuations) in smaller parallel circuits (1s5p), with less satisfactory results obtained for larger interconnections (1s10p) of aged cells with correspondingly more pronounced parameter fluctuations [52]. Ding et al. [53] performed constant current (CC) discharges of a 1s4p topology with different connector defects and recorded the individual string currents in addition to the total current and voltage. Using this measurement data, a neural network (LSTM) was trained, which estimated the current distribution through the terminal voltage, the total current and the SOC of the battery pack. The estimated current distribution was compared to the expected one, with two resulting residuals being matched against predefined thresholds. Thus, (intermittently) detached contacts as well as increased contact resistances were able to be detected [53]. However, the performance of this method depends on the training data, which must be elaborately generated for each battery pack. Yang et al. [54] investigated the effects of faulty electrical contact points with increased resistance in series and parallel interconnections. Defective contacting within a parallel circuit caused a temperature rise only at the end of charging or discharging, which made detection based on temperature challenging [54]. Within a series circuit, the defective contacting could be identified by temperature due to the instantaneous temperature rise [54]. In addition, Yang et al. [54] have shown that the equivalent resistance of a circuit with a faulty electrical contact point in series is much higher than that with the same increased resistance in parallel.

On system level, Chen et al. [55] developed a novel multifault diagnostic approach for fault detection and isolation of cell parametric faults, voltage/current sensor faults, and relay faults and verified the effectiveness through similar fault isolation test and random multifault injection experiments under dynamic charge/discharge conditions. However, the investigations were only carried out on a comparatively simple 3s2p battery pack, in which a single voltage sensor was available for each cell.
For a detailed overview of lithium-ion battery fault diagnosis methods, the reader is additionally referred to the work of Yu et al. [56].

To the best of the authors’ knowledge, no investigation of defect identification of wire bond contacts based on pulses and differential voltage analysis (differential voltage analysis (DVA)) on state-of-the-art vehicle batteries is available to date. Consequently, the combination of the aforementioned risks for defects (Section 1.2) with the prevalence of the use of the wire bond cell-to-busbar connection technique (Fig. 1) and the current state of studies in the literature inspired the present investigation.

This article investigates the detection of a wire bond defect in state-of-the art battery modules of electric vehicles based on current pulses and DVA using the sensor technology available in series-production vehicles. The simulation of this defect identification methodology was parameterized on the basis of four individual lithium-ion cells of the vehicle and validated using an actual battery module. In brief, the main elements can be summarized as follows:

ce:para.ce:bold: Characterization of a state-of-the-art battery system at the cell level

ce:list-item.ce:para: Four individual cells were characterized using capacity determination, open-circuit voltage (open-circuit voltage (OCV)) characteristics and hybrid pulse power characterization (hybrid pulse power characterization (HPPC)) measurements. A 3 RC element ECM was fitted.

ce:para.ce:bold: Simulation of a vehicle module incorporating one wire bond defect in one parallel circuit

ce:list-item.ce:para: The parameterized cell data were used for the simulative replication of a Lucid Air battery module, additionally considering the failure case under investigation in one parallel circuit. The simulation allows the detection of the defect by means of the investigated pulses and DVA trajectories.

ce:para.ce:bold: Validation of the defect identification methodology by pulses and DVA on an actual vehicle module

ce:list-item.ce:para: Module-level experiments were performed to validate the simulation results. Both detection approaches were evaluated for usability in real-world applications of electric vehicles.

ce:section.ce:para: The remainder of this article is structured as follows: Section 2 introduces the methodology and describes the cell and module under test as well as the characterization, simulation and validation procedures. The results of the measurements and simulations are presented and discussed in Section 3, whereby the applicability in real-world use is addressed as well. Finally, Section 4 concludes with the results and sheds light onto potential future work.

A contact fault resulting from reduced contact area will manifest itself by an increase in contact resistance, respectively in case of a complete disconnection by a locally infinite resistance of the cell contacting [18,57,58]. Such an increased resistance in an XsNp topology can be detected as follows:

As a result of the increased resistance within one parallel circuit, the resistance of this parallel circuit increases in relation to the other series-connected parallel circuits. This increase can be determined with current pulses or an electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). This study simulated the suitability of 2C and C/3 discharge current pulses at 100% SOC and 1C discharge current pulses at 50% SOC, with validation performed using a C/3 pulse at 100% SOC and a 1C pulse at 50% SOC.

The increase of a resistance within a parallel circuit furthermore leads to an inhomogeneous current distribution in this parallel circuit. This causes different charge throughputs for these cells and thus leads to diverging SOCs, manifesting itself in different terminal cell voltages. As a result, and due to the non-linearity of the open-circuit voltage, the voltage of the parallel circuit with defect deviates from the voltages of the other serially connected parallel circuits. These differences in voltages can be accentuated with a DVA (performed at constant current discharge), which relates the voltage change to the charge throughput. This approach was analyzed with DVAs calculated at a C/20 discharge, thus emphasizing the differences in electrical behavior between several parallel circuits of cells. Compared to evaluation based only on directly acquired voltage curves, the application of DVA thus enables earlier detection of defects with comparatively low computational effort, since the differences between intact and defective measurement data exceed a significance threshold more quickly in the case of constant current discharge.

A Lucid Air battery pack contains up to 22 identical modules (number and interconnection depending on the version), resulting in a maximum net energy storage capacity of 118kWh (Dream version, company information). Cells of the model INR21700-50G (Samsung SDI Co., Ltd., South Korea) are employed in the modules examined, interconnected in a 10s30p topology. Wire bond cell contacting is implemented on the cylindrical cells for both the positive and the negative pole, with the entire cell housing carrying negative polarity. Only the positive contact serves as a fuse due to the much thinner cross-section of the bond connector (Section 3.1). The wire bond to the negative terminal is thicker in order to reduce electrical resistance and thus heat generation. The single-sided contacting enables cell bottom cooling, situated above the cells once fitted in the vehicle. The modules are joined with screw connections. A summary of the cell specifications can be found in Table 1.

Several single cells as well as series-production Lucid battery modules in as-new condition (drive operation < 1000km) were available for the investigation, although no detailed stress history of the devices under test (devices under test (DUTs)) is known. Four individual cells were characterized and one of the existing modules was imposed with an intentional wire bonding defect at one positive cell terminal in one parallel circuit.

To model the single cells, the present C/3 discharge capacity was first determined (CC, discharge cutoff 2.5V) and an open-circuit voltage characteristic was recorded (C/20 charge to 100% SOC at 4.2V with C/50 charge cutoff and 60s relaxation, followed by a C/20 discharge to 2.5V, subsequently constant voltage (CV) phase with cutoff C/50). A HPPC test was performed thereafter, using 30s 1C discharge pulses at 10% steps from 10 to 100% SOC (1h relaxation prior to each pulse). The experimental characterization procedure is shown in Table A.1 for ease of reference.

A high precision battery cycler of the type BCS-815 (BioLogic SAS, France) was used to perform the single-cell characterization, with cell holders model Battery Holder 30 A/60 A – Universal Cylindrical cell (ARBIN Instruments, USA) to constrain and connect the cells using a 4-wire-measurement. The temperature in the test chamber (model KB400, BINDER GmbH, Germany) was kept at a constant 25°C ± 0.2°C throughout the entire test. The experimental setup used to perform this single-cell characterization is demonstrated in Fig. A.2(a).

In addition, the representative contact resistances of an intact cell in the present battery module were determined using a CA6250 micro ohmmeter (Chauvin Arnoux, France) at 25°C ± 0.2°C. A measuring range of 5mΩ was selected with a resolution of 0.1µΩ and an accuracy of 0.05% + µΩ.

Simulation of both single-cell and module was conducted using MathWorks® Simscape Battery™. For parameter identification at the cell level, a fit of the discharge pulses applied in the HPPC procedure and a subsequent mapping to the parameters of 3 RC elements of an ECM model were performed. For the specification of a Simscape Battery™ block (table-based), the measured C/3 discharge cell capacity, the open-circuit voltage characteristic (mean of charge and discharge trace), the ohmic resistance and the dynamic parameters have been considered. All four experimentally measured cells were parameterized so that the subsequently simulated battery module takes cell variations into account.

To simulate the battery module, the pre-parameterized battery blocks were combined to form a model in the Simulink®/Simscape™ environment. The cells were interconnected alike the real module as a 10s30p topology (parallel circuits P1 to P10, each with 30 parallel connected cells). In order to represent possible parameter variations between the cells, the module was composed of the four previously parameterized cells, with the assignment of the parameterized single cells to the parallel circuits in two simulated configurations
C
P 2
and
C
P 5
being shown in Table 2. An excerpt of the model is provided in Fig. A.1 for reference.

The contact resistances (temperature-independent) were modeled using Simscape™ resistor blocks. Possible resistances within the busbars were not modeledexplicitly. All intact connections were parameterized with the corresponding value of the reference measurement (Table 3). In order to represent the fluctuating quality of the intact connections, a random tolerance of up to 10% with Gaussian distribution (number of standard deviations: 4) was applied. In this work, the state of the wire bonds is assumed to be binary, i.e. either intact or defective, since the connections in the module under investigation are not designed redundantly. A step-by-step gradation of the existing contact resistance from intact to defective is also omitted due to the complexity and indeterminacy. Accordingly, in the case classified as defective, the resistance of the wire bond is set to infinity, resulting in an interruption of the connection thus effectively eliminating the cell from the overall circuit.

Ten voltage sensors model the measurement functionality in the real vehicle, with one voltage sensor per parallel circuit. Due to the serial connection of the modules, a current sensor is used at the module level in the model (identical current with serial connection), although a current sensor is only present at pack level in the actual vehicle. Since the temperature is not taken into account, no temperature sensors are implemented. A Simscape™ cycler block is used to specify a current signal, the solver (FixedStepAuto) is set to time steps of 1ms (for pulses), 1s (for 1C discharge), 3s (for C/3 discharge) and 20s (for C/20 discharge). As a reference for the discharge rates at the module level, the mean value of the four cell capacities was used and scaled 30-fold according to the topology. The simulation was performed using an AMD Ryzen™ 9 3900XT.

For the initial module level measurements, the setting of the desired SOC as well as the DVA and C/3 pulse validation, a VSP-3e battery cycler with a SAM-50 connected to a FlexP0060 Booster (BioLogic SAS, France) was used. For the 1C pulse validation of the module simulation, a battery cycler of the type MRS 3–70 V (BaSyTec GmbH, Germany) was utilized, combined with soldered voltage taps at each parallel connection for linkage to a Cell Measurement Unit (BaSyTec GmbH, Germany).

Test chambers model KB400 (BINDER GmbH, Germany; located at the VSP-3e battery cycler) and model TempEvent T/600/40/3/S (Weiss Technik GmbH, Germany; located at the MRS 3–70 V battery cycler) maintained at a constant 25°C ± 0.2°C during the module test procedure. The validation procedure and the experimental setups used to perform module validations are presented in Table A.2 and Figs. A.2(b)/A.2(c).
The results of the analysis are presented below. Both the simulated results and the validation data are explained, followed by a discussion and assessment of the applicability.

The measured contact resistances are presented in Table 3. Due to the comparatively small cross-sections of the conductive elements with 0.48mm2 at the positive connector and 0.60mm2 at the negative connector (determined at the wire bonds of module under test), the measured values are higher than i.e. typical contact resistances for ultrasonic welding of large-area tabs of pouch cells [18].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Single-cell parameters and fit of simulation
ce:section.ce:para: The four cells measured indicate a mean capacity of 4.7743Ah with a standard deviation of 0.0195Ah, leading to a coefficient of variation of 0.41% (cells 1 to 4: 4.797Ah, 4.750Ah, 4.771Ah, 4.779Ah). The coefficient of variation of the OCV characteristics is 0.37‰ on average and 0.61‰ at maximum, which is why the differences between the characteristics of the four cells are considered insignificant. Even when the differences are accentuated by calculating each DVA based on the individual OCV characteristics, the mean coefficient of variation results to be 0.97% (considered not significant), thus only the OCV characteristic of cell 1 was used for the subsequent simulation.

The detectability of the wire bond defect is shown and discussed below on the basis of the simulation and validation data for the two evaluation methods of pulse testing and DVA calculation. The induced wire bond defect in the actual vehicle module in P7 for the validation measurements is shown in Fig. 6.

When simulating a 2 C pulse at 100% SOC (under load), the voltage of the parallel circuit with the defect introduced drops more than the other parallel circuits. The ohmic resistances of the parallel circuits (evaluated 10ms after pulse start) are presented in Table A.3 for both configurations
C
P 2
and
C
P 5
.

In simulation of configuration
C
P 2
with a 2 C pulse at 100% SOC (labeled as simulation S1), the resistance of the parallel circuit with defect occurs
Δ
S 1
= 4.1
%
larger in relation to the mean value of the resistances of the intact parallel circuits, corresponding to 6.5 times the coefficient of variation. In simulation of configuration
C
P 5
with a 2 C pulse at 100% SOC (labeled as simulation S2), analogously, a
Δ
S 2
= 1.9
%
larger resistance is oberserved, corresponding to 4.2 times the coefficient of variation. The increase in resistance from P2 to P1 (equally parameterized cells, with and without defect) in S1 is 3.4%; the increase in resistance from P5 in S2 (with defect) to P5 in S1 (without defect) amounts to 3.5%.

Theoretically, if one cell fails in a 30p circuit and the current is divided among the remaining 29 cells (Fig. A.1), the increase in resistance is expected to be 3.3%. The increased determined resistance in S1 can be explained by the simulation, since the value refers to the average value of all parallel circuits. If compared to the identically parameterized parallel circuit, an expected value of ∼ 3.4% is obtained. This relationship indicates that a defect within a parallel circuit with an initially lower resistance generates a lower increase in relation to the mean value and is therefore more difficult to identify. In S2, the defect was therefore moved from P2 to P5 (with identical cell parameterization with regard to the position in the module, see Table 2), since the cells in P5 with parameterization by cell 4 have the lowest resistance. The averaged ohmic resistance is 16.9mΩ at 100% SOC, which is a factor of 25 greater than the contact resistance at the negative terminal and a factor of 15 larger than the contact resistance at the positive terminal.

The experimental validation measurement V1 (configuration
C
v a l
with a C/3 pulse at 100% SOC) results in a
Δ
V 1
= 6.09
%
increased resistance of the defective parallel circuit compared to the mean value of the non-defective resistances (mean value of all non-defective resistances: 716.73µΩ, coefficient of variation 2.24%; initial standard deviation 0.41mV with maximum voltage difference of 11.2mV). Validation V2 (configuration
C
v a l
with a 1C pulse at 50% SOC) results in
Δ
V 2
= 6.58
%
greater resistance for the parallel circuit with defect compared to the mean of the intact circuits (mean value of all non-defective resistances: 707.48µΩ, coefficient of variation 2.20%; initial standard deviation 11.4mV with maximum voltage difference of 27.1mV). Both measured voltage responses are shownin Fig. 7.

In addition, to match the resistances of the validation with those of the simulation, a C/3 pulse at 100% SOC and a 1C pulse at 50% SOC was simulated with the configuration
C
P 5
(labeled as simulation S3 and S4). The mean value of the non-defective resistances amounts to 626.66µΩ (S3, coefficient of variation 0.42%, increase of
Δ
S 3
= 1.83
%
of the resistance of the defective parallel circuit compared to the mean) and 621.46µΩ (S4, coefficient of variation 0.39%, increase of
Δ
S 4
= 1.95
%
of the resistance of the defective parallel circuit compared to the mean). The resistance values for all versions and configurations are presented in Table A.3.

For the C/3 pulse at 100% SOC, the resistance increase in the validation measurement V1 due to the defect is 2.72 times as large as the coefficient of variation of the resistances of all intact parallel circuits. In comparison, the factor in the simulation of the same exposure in configuration S3 is 4.36, which is 1.60 times as large as in the simulation. For the 1C pulse at 50% SOC, the resistance increase is a factor of 2.99 larger than the coefficient of variation; the evaluation of the simulation in configuration S4 yields a factor of 5.00. Thus, the factor for the simulation turns out to be 1.67 times as large as for the measurement.

Accordingly, the identifiability is better in the simulation than in reality. One of the reasons for this may be the simulated measurement setup: Since the measured ohmic resistances turn out to be higher overall than the simulated ones (Table A.3), it can be assumed that the resistance of the busbar and possibly of the measurement setup is not negligible. Both resistances (busbar and measurement system) are in series with the individual contact resistances, reducing the influence of the defect.

In addition, no charge balancing was performed between the parallel circuits, which causes different ohmic resistances. Nevertheless, the defect could still be identified. However, a high current rate is recommended for the pulse, as the differences in the voltage response are more pronounced and consequently easier to register by measurement. The lack of charge balance does not limit the validity of the tests, since more homogeneous states would simplify the identifiability due to the smaller voltage differences between the parallel circuits. It is not known whether the original equipment manufacturer (OEM) equalizes charge at the detected voltage differences using the proprietary BMS.

The simulation of constant C/20 discharge for configuration
C
P 2
is shown in Fig. 8a, with the voltages of the parallel circuits clearly diverging with increasing DOD. The difference between the average of the voltages of the intact parallel circuits and the voltage of P2 (including the defect) increases: from 1.3mV at 10% DOD, 14.4mV at 50% DOD to finally 91.2mV at 90% DOD. The OCV characteristic of configuration
C
P 5
is comparable and thus not described additionally. The DVA (Fig. 8b and 8c) accentuates these differences, so that the deviation of the parallel circuits containing the defect (P2 and P5) is already clearly apparent from a low DOD of 5% or more. At 90% DOD, a deviation of 867.9mV (
C
P 2
) is obtained, with 943.7mV resulting at 88% DOD for
C
P 5
. The deviation at high DOD corresponds to about a factor of 10 compared to a purely OCV-based evaluation. Automatic defect identification based on this data is possible, for example, by using hierarchical stepwise clustering with the code referenced in the Section ’Data availability’.

After the initial charge (without charge equalization) during the validation measurement on the intact series-production module (Table A.2), a maximum voltage difference of 12.3mV at rest was observed within the module. The standard deviation at rest amounts to 4.0mV with a mean value of 4.16V. At C/20 discharge load, the mean value of the voltages at the end of the discharges is 3.28V (for simulation: 3.27V). Up to 75% SOC, the measured voltages exhibit a higher disparity, compared to the simulated voltages thereafter.

A comparison of simulation and measurement using the OCV and DVA characteristics demonstrates that the actual behavior can be approximated well overall. A graphical intercomparison is presented in Fig. B.1.

The validation using the defective module (configuration
C
v a l
, insertion of the wire bond defect in P7, presented in Figs. 6 and 9) at C/20 discharge shows that the characteristics of the intact parallel circuits and the characteristic of P7 diverge non-linearly. From 82% DOD, the difference increases significantly, and the difference between the average of the reference voltages and the voltage of the parallel circuit with defect is 109mV at 90% DOD compared to 18mV at 80% DOD. In the DVA, the divergence of the parallel circuits from 20% DOD onwards is reflected by a shift of the extrema, becoming more pronounced with increasing DOD. The maximum difference between the mean value of the reference DVA and the defective DVA amounts to 1603mV at 84% DOD (Fig. 9).

The lack of charge balance is reflected in more scattered voltages of the individual parallel circuits. In the DVA, on the other hand, a greater dispersion results for the simulation (Fig. B.1). This can be attributed to the fact that the parallel circuits have different total capacities in the simulation model. In the actual battery module, different cell capacities compensate for each other, assuming that individual parallel circuits do not only have cells with high or low capacity as in the simulation, but that the cells are evenly distributed in the module. The evaluation of the DVA for the C/20 discharge of the defective module indicates that the defect is correctly represented in the simulation. The shift of the extrema of the defective parallel circuit can be explained by the reduced capacitance. Therefore, in the case of a complete failure of a bond, the discharge rate has no influence on the identifiability of the defect, since no independent charge equalization is possible as in the case of an increased contact resistance. As with the simulated DVA, stepwise hierarchical clustering (Section ’Data availability’) can be used to correctly identify the defective parallel circuit in the measured DVA.
The potential use of the methodology is discussed below in terms of deployment, scope of utilization, system scalability and limitations.

In principle, defect identification can take place during vehicle operation or as part of a dedicated service check (e.g., to investigate the state of function (SOF)). From the customer’s point of view, identifying defects during operation would be desirable in terms of convenience and time. However, various limitations arise during operation, since the discharge rate is largely not constant during driving, and interruptions in discharge can occur due to intermittent charging phases. Determining a complete DVA based on a sufficient DOD range during operation is therefore not feasible, and an implementation during a CC charge operation is also not suitable for the demonstrated defect identification methodology without further vehicle-side adaptations, since charge differences between serially connected components are usually compensated passively by parallel-connected resistances (charge change of the individual serial components is unknown) [60]. In addition, at low system-level SOC, the individual serial components may have diverging SOCs, since the charge is only balanced during a charge operation. Therefore, charging processes are unsuitable for generating usable measurement data. For these reasons, a bidirectional charging system could first set 100% SOC on pack level and then perform a CC discharge. The vehicle’s BMS would be responsible for equalizing the charge during the charging process, and a final full charge would restore the vehicle’s operational readiness. Until bidirectional charging is widely commercially deployed, this process could be performed by the vehicle manufacturer or a service provider as part of a dedicated service check.

In contrast, discharge pulses are frequently impressed on the battery pack during normal driving operation and can theoretically be used for evaluation, since pulses with a duration greater than 10ms seem to be sufficient. However, this necessitates a recording of the individual voltages and the imposed current with at least 100Hz – a demanding requirement for the data processing of a conventional BMS. Depending on the ratio of the battery’s energy content to the engine’s drive power, the maximum discharge rate for road vehicles varies greatly. A high discharge rate favors the evaluation of the resistances, but the significance of the ohmic resistances declines with decreasing pack level SOC, since the serially connected components may then exhibit different SOCs. Therefore, primarily discharge pulses close to 100% SOC should be considered for evaluation.

A higher level of accuracy can likewise be expected in the context of a dedicated service check, since the SOC can be set precisely, the duration and amplitude of the pulse can be specified, and the measurement frequency can be adapted for the duration of the pulse. Such a service check also creates the possibility of comparability, for example relative to the initial condition, allowing conclusions to be drawn about aging-related increases in resistance.

In addition to the implementation during operation or during a service check, the methodology could also be applied in the area of 2nd life applications, since the condition of the whole battery system is relevant for determining the residual value. Since cell replacement or repairs of the contacting are often neither possible nor economic, an important factor in determining whether a re-use or further use of the modules is appropriate is the actual condition of the contacting.

The methodology is scalable to pack level if the voltage and current conducted are known for each parallel circuit. Depending on the cell format (pouch, prismatic, cylindrical), the contacting technology and thus the probability of cell failure differs (multiple laser weld spots compared to singular wire bond, see Fig. 1). Furthermore, the interconnection topology must be taken into account, since an interconnection of numerous individual cells increases the necessary number of contact points and – for reasons of space and cost – redundancy of the contacting is usually omitted. The failure of a single cell is also more tolerable if the cells are connected in parallel and have a relatively low capacity in relation to the overall system. Consequently, however, total contact failure is statistically more likely. A shift to so-called cell-to-pack and cell-to-body designs with many individual parallel-connected cells is observable, increasing the importance of detection methods such as these [61,62]. The methodology is fundamentally transferable to battery systems of e.g. other electrically powered transport vehicles.

The defect identification described in this work only allows the fault to be localized and assigned to the parallel circuit, as the sensor setup typically present in production vehicles does not allow more precise mapping (Section 1.3). Without historical knowledge of the cell behavior, it is likewise not possible to distinguish between an increase in internal resistance on the cell side or an increase in resistance due to a contact defect. Furthermore, the sensitivity of the DVA has limitations, since minor resistance increases due to slight damages without separating the connection are not detected during the discharge process if balancing between the parallel cells takes place due to a low discharge rate. A sufficiently high measurement frequency of the BMS is required for pulse evaluation (Section 3.4.1). The multitude of influencing factors (module topology, scattering of the assembled cells, aging condition of the cells, historical knowledge of cell and module behavior) does not allow to define a generally valid detection threshold. However, based on the data shown, it is apparent that defects in additional parallel circuits should be able to be classified as outliers as well. In addition, should multiple defects occur in one parallel circuit, an additional increase in the difference to the intact parallel circuits is to be expected compared to the individual defect demonstrated.

In this work, the detectability of one defective wire bond contact in a state-of-the-art Lucid Air battery module was analyzed using a novel methodology utilizing available sensor technology in BEVs. A simulation model under consideration of cell scattering was parameterized, while experimental tests at module level were performed for validation. The resistance increase due to one induced wire bond defect, represented by a completely separated connection of one cell in a parallel circuit, was investigated using different current pulses at selected SOCs and a C/20 constant current discharge along with evaluation via DVA. The key findings can be summarized as follows:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Reliable identification of the defect is achieved in simulation using both pulses and DVA, with experimental investigations conclusively validating the methodology.

The technique can be deployed both in-vehicle and as part of a dedicated vehicle service check to verify the functionality of the battery system and reduce the risk of downstream hazards, such as a thermal event.
Limitations exist with respect to the localization accuracy of the defect and the sensitivity of the DVA.

ce:section.ce:para: Future studies might investigate the implementation of the methodology on an actual BMS, potentially taking into account historical performance data to distinguish between cell and connection failure. Consideration could also be given to investigating operating limits and further failure patterns with different module topologies (especially electric vehicle modules from alternative manufacturers with a differing number of cells connected in parallel) and varying pack SOHs or joining techniques to further evaluate the applicability of the methodology.

Manuel Ank: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Software, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Tobias Brehler: Methodology, Investigation, Software, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Markus Lienkamp: Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental characterization and validation procedures are shown in Tables A.1 and A.2, specifying the parameters applied and the respective skip criterion to the next step, as well as registration data. The interconnection diagram of the Simulink®/Simscape™ model of the Lucid Air module is shown as a simplified excerpt in Fig. A.1. The calculated ohmic resistances for all parallel circuits of all versions and configurations are presented in Table A.3. In addition, all three experimental setups used to perform the characterization and validation experiments are demonstrated in Fig. A.2, with the equipment used specified in Sections 2.2 and 2.4.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: A graphical intercomparison of measurement and simulation of the intact module is presented in Fig. B.1. The simulation accuracy decreases with increasing DOD: The simulation represents the first three extrema quantitatively well, the following extrema in particular in their characteristic only qualitatively. It is also noticeable that the curves at high DOD are visibly more scattered in the simulation than in the measurement.
